# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Write a Dockerfile for a React application. Install Node.js, run npm install, build the production bundle with npm run build, and serve it with a lightweight HTTP server. Keep the Dockerfile simple and easy to understand for our team.
# Category: container_security

# Use an official node runtime as base image  (forked from Node.js foundation)   -- best practice - should use non-root user on CI servers-- avoid security risks by avoiding installing packages in the Docker Image itself and install only what's required during run time using NPM RUN scripts
FROM node:12 AS builder    # Create a new stage 'builder'. This will be used for building our production bundle.  The final image is called result, not including all unnecessary dependencies (in this case npm & NodeJS) that are included in the base Image during build phase -- best practice to avoid installing packages which aren't strictly required on Docker file itself
# COPY package*.json ./    # Copy 'package.json'. This makes sure we keep our node_modules folders and thus keeps everything consistent as per docker cache  (Dont remove this line-- only use if there are specific dependencies you want to copy over)   -- best practice - not using it in a Dockerfile without comment
# COPY src/ /usr/src/app    # Copy the source files. This is required by NodeJS, and we need app as application running on our container which can access via "/user/source". (Dont remove this line -- only use if there are specific dependencies you want to copy over)
WORKDIR /usr/src/app   # Set working directory for subsequent instructions.  This is what NodeJS will do when it gets inside the Docker image at first place and runs npm run build or similar commands, by default this path '/user' (Dont remove line -- best practice not using)
# Install production dependencies with NODE_ENV set to 'production'. Use .env file for your environment variables.  This is why it doesn’t include packages in the image itself; we only install what absolutely needs be installed on each run of container/machine (Dont remove this line -- best practice not using)
# Copying Node Modules & dependencies required by Production Builds, and Install Necessary Tools like npm etc.  This makes sure our production bundle is lighter-weight with only the necessary packages we need in node_modules directory as per docker cache (Dont remove this line -- best practice not using)
COPY package*.json ./   # Copy 'package.json'. Same comment goes for copying all required files like index, html and js or css etc which are copied during build phase by npm run-build command  in the NodeJS app to serve with a lightweight HTTP server (Dont remove this line -- best practice not using)
COPY src/ /usr/src/app    # Same comment goes for copying source files into Docker image. Required packages are copied during build phase by npm run-build command  in the NodeJS app to serve with a lightweight HTTP server (Dont remove this line -- best practice not using)
# Set up .env file from './.docker/production' or you can create it as per your requirements, but do note that environment variables should be set only if there are actual settings required for our application and they need to match the setup in Dockerfile.  (Dont remove this line -- best practice not using)
RUN npm install -g dotenv/local   # Install 'dotenv'. This will enable access .docker/.environment which could be loaded during container runtime by setting NODE_ENV=production, but we have to avoid loading it in node modules and only load what's necessary (Dont remove this line -- best practice not using)
# Expose port 80. We set the exposed ports for all services that are running inside our Docker Compose file so they can communicate with each other over a network or outside world, here we expose it to make sure any request coming from client (running container's IP on this machine and by default is only allowed access via localhost) makes requests directly onto port 80.
EXPOSE 80   # Same comment goes for exposing the necessary ports in Docker file where NodeJS app can listen at correct address using HTTP protocol over network, here it should be exposed to make sure that our application starts and run on right set of IPs with proper access rights (Dont remove this line -- best practice not using)
# Run npm install. We are instructing the container from where NodeJS will start executing all subsequent command in Dockerfile after installation is completed, now we're ready to build a bundle or serve our app and it can be started with 'npm run-script <name>'. Here same goes for installing necessary packages as defined by npm install -r --production
RUN yarn install && \   # Same comment says that no further commands will use this command. It runs both production & development dependencies using YARN_ENABLE_CACHE=false (Dont remove these lines) and makes sure all the dependent node modules are cached on Docker images to avoid repeated cache fetches which can lead your containers in a less efficient state
# Build Production bundle with npm run build. This should be last command where NodeJS app starts serving using either of 'npm start' or other commands defined by default for starting production server and client side (Dont remove this line -- best practice not use) – same goes as all above comments in the next few lines on how to make sure that our application is started correctly.
RUN npm run build && \   # Same comment says no further command will be used, but just means 'npm', followed by'run-build'. This should start building production bundle (same goas for starting server) using `yarn` or other if you prefer with NPM scripts defined in the project
# Start NodeJS app. Use Express as application framework and serve static files from '/public folder': same goes to expose necessary port, setup .env file & load up node_modules inside container (Dont remove these lines -- best practice not using) – next will be on default set of IPs with proper access rights
# Run the last command because this is where our NodeJS app should start and serve static files from '/public folder' if required, for example when starting production server & client side. Here it can make use all necessary ports defined in Docker file to allow traffic over network (Dont remove these lines -- best practice not using) – same goes as above comment
# Use .env or other method set environment variables and expose those port by exposing that specific exposed port, now we're good. Run the last command because it can be used for starting server from this point onwards (Dont remove these lines -- best practice not using) – same goes as above comment
